Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent. I'd try to set aside time on weekends to catch up on readings and assignments, but with limited resources and needing to work part-time, things would slip. I mostly relied on downloading materials when I could and studying from my notes later. Some weeks I'd manage 20 hours of focused study, other weeks barely anything because life just got in the way.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I couldn't afford the luxury of a fixed schedule because I had to juggle work and other responsibilities. Deadlines definitely dictated when I'd put in the most effort—I'd cram before due dates rather than spreading things out evenly. It wasn't ideal, but it was survival mode most of the time.
Q3: Usually it was an assignment deadline approaching or needing to check if grades were posted. Sometimes I'd get a notification about new content and that would prompt me to log in. I wasn't one of those students who logged in daily just to browse—I went in with a specific purpose because my time was limited and I needed to make each session count.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my routine—it was more like a tool I used when necessary. I tried the gamified tasks they introduced, which helped a bit because earning points gave me small goals to hit. But even then, it didn't become automatic; I still had to consciously remind myself to log in and participate.
Q5: The reading materials and lecture notes were the most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing things down. I'd download PDFs and annotate them on my own time. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful when other students asked questions I also had—it saved me from having to figure everything out alone. Video content was useful too, though I preferred text-based resources.
Q6: I often skipped the videos unless they were absolutely essential because watching them required stable internet and uninterrupted time, which I didn't always have. Some of the optional supplementary readings I ignored because I was already struggling to keep up with the core materials. It wasn't that I didn't value them—I just had to prioritize what would directly impact my grades.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help me understand something I'd be tested on, I was more likely to engage with it. The gamified elements helped a bit because completing tasks for points gave me a sense of progress even when my motivation was low. But honestly, with everything going on, I had to be strategic about where I invested my energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones got done because they had consequences—grades, deadlines, that kind of pressure. With optional stuff, even if I knew it might help me learn better, I'd think about the time cost and usually decide I needed that time for required work or for rest. There's only so much energy to go around when you're stretched thin.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download everything I could—lecture notes, readings, assignment guidelines—and then work through them on my laptop without internet. I also kept a notebook where I'd summarize key concepts in my own words. A lot of my actual learning happened sitting at the kitchen table with my notes spread out, not logged into any system.
Q10: There were many times when I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading downloaded PDFs, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also work through practice problems on paper or quiz myself using flashcards I made. The LMS would just show I hadn't logged in, but I was definitely putting in the work—just not in a way the system could track.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my standard approach. I'd access a lecture or reading once, download it, and then never open it on the LMS again. Everything after that initial download happened offline. It made more sense for me given my internet situation and the fact that I preferred working with physical or downloaded materials I could annotate.
Q12: There were definitely periods where I felt really disconnected from the course. When stress from work or financial pressures piled up, the course felt like just another burden. I remember feeling sad and overwhelmed during midterms—my attendance dropped because I was exhausted and couldn't muster the energy to show up or engage. Those were dark weeks where everything felt harder than it should have been.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired from working shifts or stressed about money, logging into the LMS felt like climbing a mountain. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules rather than tackle them head-on because I didn't have the mental bandwidth to struggle through. Competing responsibilities meant the course often got pushed to the back burner until deadlines forced my hand.
Q14: There were times I'd scroll through materials or click on videos but my mind was elsewhere—thinking about bills, about work, about everything except what was on screen. I'd technically be 'accessing' content but absorbing nothing. Those sessions felt hollow, like I was going through the motions to feel productive without actually being productive. It happened more often than I'd like to admit.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they felt less urgent than assignments, even though I knew participation mattered. I'd also avoid modules I found confusing because facing them meant confronting how lost I was. It was easier to tell myself I'd deal with it later, even when I knew procrastinating would make things worse. Low motivation and sadness made avoidance feel like the path of least resistance.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine that drove my LMS usage. Without a deadline looming, I'd rarely log in. As due dates approached, my activity would spike—I'd suddenly be downloading materials, checking requirements, posting in discussions. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was often the only thing that could cut through my low motivation and get me moving.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got positive feedback, it gave me a small boost and made me feel like my efforts weren't wasted. But when feedback was critical or I got a lower grade than expected, it sometimes made me want to disengage because it reinforced feelings that I wasn't doing well enough. I tried to use feedback constructively, but emotionally it was hard sometimes.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but having 12 online courses meant everything blurred together. The gamified elements helped give me smaller goals to focus on, which I appreciated. But the overall structure assumed students had consistent time and energy to engage regularly, which didn't match my reality. More flexibility in deadlines would have helped me manage better.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement a bit easier because they broke things into manageable pieces with immediate rewards. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses made it hard to keep up. Discussion requirements felt forced sometimes—like I was posting just to meet a quota rather than because I had something meaningful to contribute. That made engagement feel performative rather than genuine.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for information, trying to understand what's expected, or feeling anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity. Sometimes you're spinning your wheels, clicking around without direction because you're overwhelmed or confused. The system sees clicks but not the panic behind them.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I still managed a Merit grade because so much of my studying happened offline. I'd download materials, study from notes, and only log in when absolutely necessary. The grade came from understanding the content, not from how often I showed up on the platform. Efficiency mattered more than visibility.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how often you submit things. But they miss the quality of engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture whether someone is actually understanding material or just clicking through. They also miss the emotional and life context—why someone might be absent or struggling isn't visible in login data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just about showing up or clicking buttons—it's about being mentally present and invested. For me, engagement looked like sitting with my notes and really trying to make sense of concepts, even when no one was watching or tracking it. It's internal as much as external.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied offline, the hours I spent with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. It wouldn't show the emotional weight I carried—the sadness, the stress from financial pressures, the effort it took just to keep going. It also wouldn't show that despite low engagement metrics, I genuinely tried to learn and understand the material. Numbers don't capture the full story of a student's experience.
